chunk_summary: Counselling on safe texture progression and choking prevention emphasizes timely texture introduction, distinguishing gagging from choking, and modifying high-risk foods to prevent aspiration and choking in young children.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of timely texture progression to prevent feeding difficulties and choking risks.
Learn to differentiate between gagging and choking during feeding and respond appropriately.
Identify high-risk foods for young children and apply safe preparation and supervision strategies.
Recognize signs of feeding safety issues requiring referral for swallow assessment.
key_facts: Lumpy or granular foods should be introduced by 9–10 months when developmentally ready.
Gagging is noisy and involves effective coughing, while choking is often silent and requires immediate action.
High-risk foods include whole grapes, nuts, popcorn, hard candies, and raw carrot sticks.
Modify high-risk foods by cutting lengthwise, cooking until soft, and supervising meals.
Children should eat seated and supervised; avoid eating while walking or playing.
Forced feeding increases the risk of aspiration and feeding aversion.
reference_quote: Teach caregivers to distinguish gagging from choking. Gagging is common during texture learning and is noisy, with effective coughing and breathing. Choking is often silent or associated with inability to cough or breathe effectively and requires immediate action and prevention strategies. (Source: HealthyChildren.org)
